## [00:00 - 03:03]

Welcome back. In the last training, we really started to lay out the foundation of your event. And now in this session, I'm going to start showing you the process of transforming your event concept into something tangible and unforgettable. So get ready to unleash your creativity, showcase your expertise, and really start creating an event that resonates deeply with your audience. Now, let's dive in. The asset that will truly start creating your event is your event agenda. Carefully crafting your event agenda is a crucial component of any event, as it will serve as the blueprint for success. It's essential that each piece of your agenda is intentional and moves your attendees toward the desired outcome of your event. Remember, those are the goals that we laid out when defining the five W's of your event in the last training. A well-crafted agenda should find balance between speakers, interactive sessions, Q&A, networking opportunities, and breaks. Remember, live events should be dynamic. We want to include multiple opportunities for your attendees to get out of their seats, network with each other, and take a break. This will really help your attendees stay engaged and energized throughout the entire event. Now, Dean provided an outline for a mastermind agenda in a previous training, which is a great option. But let's walk through another option of a one-day agenda that you could choose. I want to help illustrate how you could structure your event for maximum impact and engagement. Remember to keep your brand in mind and adjust this agenda to what makes sense for you. Gigi is a great asset to use when doing this. Now, let's go over to the whiteboard. So, you'll see here, I have laid out an agenda for a full-day event, that you can take this and make it your own, but let's walk through each facet and why it matters. So, before your event kick starts at, let's say, 9am, you'll want to do a check-in. This is a great opportunity to engage with your attendees as they arrive and set the tone for the day. I'll talk through this process in more detail in the next training, but just keep it in mind. Then, once you actually start the event at 9am, this is where you will set up the goal for the event and what the day will really look like. You'll also want the attendees to do the same for themselves. Make them establish what their goals are for the event and where they're at right now. And this will seamlessly transition into your next session, which is the networking breakouts. That will happen for about 30 minutes where you open up the room for them to discuss their goals for the event and introduce themselves to each other. In a previous training, Dean shared with you the intro card, which is a great opportunity to use this. And then, after those conclude, you'll want to make sure you take a quick 15-minute break. It's really important to give your guests breaks between big segments to avoid overwhelm and make sure that they stay engaged. After that, you will go to your first speaker. This is where you'll have someone share their knowledge with the room, whether it's you, an outside expert you bring in, or one of your own attendees. The speaker should either share an opportunity in business that has brought them success or an obstacle they're currently struggling with and

## [03:03 - 06:05]

how they overcame it. Remember to use Story Teach tool in these moments. After that speaker has shared and done a Q&A, this is a great opportunity to do another networking breakout. You'll open up the room to discuss any aha moments or breakthroughs that they had during the speaker session, which will seamlessly transition into lunch. You'll notice that I have this for about 90 minutes, and I would encourage you to keep these longer, as these are great opportunities for your attendees to continue to network. It's another reason why we love to provide catering, so guests don't have to leave the venue and can continue networking with each other. And now we're in the afternoon. Well, you'll have another speaker join and share their knowledge with the room and provide an opportunity for Q&A. Then you'll have another break in between your last speaker of the day, because you'll want to make sure that they are able to use the restroom, grab a snack, and be fully locked into the next speaking slot, which is where your keynote speaker should be. And this is where you'll offer opportunities for Q&A with you, and this is really like the meat of your training for the day. After that keynote speaker has gone, you'll want to close out the day, and this is a great opportunity to call for breakthroughs and aha moments that your attendees have had. A question that I personally like to use is, who or what has had an impact on you today? You can really get some incredible testimonials in this moment. Then you'll just close out the day and recap what they've accomplished. And then, if it makes sense for your event, it's completely optional. But sometimes it's nice to have a second location to offer a more facilitated networking opportunity with food and drink through your networking mixer. Now, you'll notice on this agenda that there are multiple opportunities for different speakers. Not every mastermind will require outside speakers. It may just be you, or even your attendees who share at the event. However, you should meet with every speaker, even if it is your own attendees, to talk through the goal of the event. You want to make sure everyone is on the same page so that you can work together to achieve the goal of the event in every session. No one should be on your stage without a clear understanding of your end goal and expectations. When every speaker is aligned with your end goal, you create a cohesive and impactful event experience that your attendees will notice. You'll also want to create a few other event assets to enhance and support your attendees experience. These can include teaching aids, networking aids, gifts, and a few other miscellaneous items that I'll go through. Let's start with teaching aids. These can include any visual items such as slides, handouts, or just any other teaching materials. These items can play a crucial role in facilitating interactive learning and engagement from your audience. For your peace of mind, I recommend getting these from speakers before the event begins. Next is networking aids. One of the main reasons to host a mastermind is the networking that happens at the event. And as an event planner, it is your responsibility to help encourage this networking. You can do so by providing a few different resources. Name tags are a great way to encourage

## [06:05 - 08:15]

networking between your attendees. That way, no one feels too shy to introduce themselves. Table tents are another great asset, not only to help your attendees know where they're sitting, but who they're sitting next to. Plus, if a speaker isn't as familiar with the room, they can see and say their name, which really helps with the intimacy of the room. And the last networking asset is intro cards. Now, that may sound familiar, as I mentioned that when I was going through the agenda, but these can really help provide a structured opportunity for your attendees to share who they are and why they're there. Another asset to think through providing is thoughtful gifts upon your attendees' arrival. This helps just add a personal touch to the event experience for your guests. Gifts such as branded journals, pens, or water bottles are great. Personally, I love theming gifts to where we're hosting the event. For example, if we're in a colder environment, I like to provide jackets or blankets. Or if we're in a beach location, I'll provide towels and sunscreen. Just have fun with it. There are also some other miscellaneous assets you could add to your event to make it even smoother if it makes sense for your event. For example, waiver and registration forms. These protect both you and the attendee by outlining important logistical details and agreements. Our waivers typically cover logistical items such as filming, photography, content shared, and promoting guidelines as well as food allergies. I'll make sure that we provide a template for you to review and make your own. Wi-Fi codes are another great asset to provide. Typically, you wouldn't think about it, but your attendees will ask. We like to print these out and display them around the venue for guests to connect on their own so that way they don't have to ask. Another asset is worksheets that can support your speakers.
